body:
Updates the project configuration for current .NET tooling and refreshes the GitHub workflows around it.

## Changes
### Hygiene
- add central package version management
- replace the legacy `.sln` with `.slnx` and let the provider find either format
- update CI and NuGet package publishing workflows
- multi-TFM targets for both LTS .NET targets `net8.0;net10.0` + `netstandard2.0` for the packages, as there is nothing holding this extra compatibility back
### Fixes for broken tests
- avoid blocking RPC calls from test plugin callbacks
- exercise UI redraw notification dispatch without depending on `nvim_ui_attach` (this caused tests to fail to stop)

## Upgrade
> [!WARNING]
> Package publishing will need a `NUGET_API_KEY` project secret before the release workflow can push to NuGet. Ideally this should target the existing `neovim` NuGet organization if ownership can be recovered; it appears to have last published in 2018. If that organization is unrecoverable, creating a new `nvim` NuGet organization would be a reasonable fallback. I would avoid publishing a package from this branch yet, since further changes are planned for standard .NET namespacing, publishing the client package itself, and aligning versioning with the Neovim version it is built against.

## Validation
- local GitHub Actions runner: `dotnet test test/NvimClient.Test/NvimClient.Test.csproj --configuration Release --framework net10.0 --no-build --blame-hang --blame-hang-timeout 60s` passes 43/43 tests
- PR `Build and test` check passes

comment_data.body: Why rename the file? It also looks like there's other unrelated formatting changes in here that make it harder to see the diff.
Are you intending to rename the default branch? Let's leave that for a separate PR, assuming we even want to spend time on it.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1,36 +1,55 @@
-name: Test
+name: CI
 
 on:
-  push:
-    branches: [ master ]
   pull_request:
-    branches: [ master ]
+  push:
+    branches:
+      - main
+      - master
+  workflow_dispatch:

comment_data.body: Yeah, good catch. I'll revert this part.

Absolutely not intending to rename the default branch. My usual default branch is `master`, I just ripped a lot of this from what I wrote for my workplace as a reusable job, where we _did_ have both names in different projects. I kept this in as it is harmless support for both standard names (i.e. git itself is moving to `main` as its default for new projects). Happy to narrow to `master` if you'd like.

comment_data.body: When trying to get the tests working instead of hanging, I found that removing this did fix one of the tests, although I can't remember exactly which one it fixed. A few of them were hanging on my machine.

Fair question. There's much more than just formatting changes here. I rewrote the CI job from scratch using a more modern deployment pattern (mostly ripped from the ci/cd suite I created in gitlab for my workplace).

I renamed the file to be more specific. I admit this was taking a liberty, but it does make the workflow target explicit, as it _is_ just publishing to nuget.

The review unit is the new CI job as a whole, not just "a fix". If the maintainers would prefer I don't rename it here, I can move this to a larger refactor I have planned to use

> but it does make the workflow target explicit, as it _is_ just publishing to nuget.

True, but I don't think that's an advantage here. If we ever publish two other targets, we likely still will do them all from the same workflow file.

> > but it does make the workflow target explicit, as it _is_ just publishing to nuget.
> 
> True, but I don't think that's an advantage here. If we ever publish two other targets, we likely still will do them all from the same workflow file.

Fair enough, I'll revert the rename.
why is `packages: write` needed now but wasn't before?

@@ -1,39 +1,93 @@
-name: Publish GitHub NuGet package
+name: Publish NuGet packages
 
 on:
-  # no triggers (manual)
   workflow_dispatch:
+    inputs:
+      package-version:
+        description: "Package version to publish"
+        required: false
+        type: string
 
 jobs:
   publish:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 
-    steps:
-      - uses: actions/checkout@v2
+    permissions:
+      contents: read
+      packages: write
 
-      - name: Install nvim binary
-        uses: rhysd/action-setup-vim@v1
-        if: steps.cache-nvim.outputs.cache-hit != 'true' || steps.cache-nvim-windows.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
-        with:
-          neovim: true
-          version: nightly
+    env:
+      CONFIGURATION: Release
+      PACKAGE_PROJECT: src/NvimClient.API/NvimClient.API.csproj
+      PACKAGE_OUTPUT: artifacts/packages
+      GITHUB_PACKAGE_SOURCE: https://nuget.pkg.github.com/neovim/index.json
+      NUGET_ORG_SOURCE: https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json
+      PACKAGE_VERSION: ${{ inputs.package-version }}
+
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout
+        uses: actions/checkout@v6
 
       - name: Setup .NET
-        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v1.8.1
+        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v5
         with:
-          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
+          dotnet-version: 10.0.x
 
-      - name: Install dependencies
+      - name: Restore
         run: dotnet restore
 
       - name: Build
-        run: dotnet build --no-restore
+        run: >
+          dotnet build
+          --configuration "$CONFIGURATION"
+          --no-restore
+          -p:ContinuousIntegrationBuild=true
+
+      - name: Pack
+        run: |
+          mkdir -p "$PACKAGE_OUTPUT"
 
-      - name: Test
-        run: dotnet test --no-build --verbosity normal test/NvimClient.Test/NvimClient.Test.csproj
+          VERSION_ARG=""
+          if [ -n "$PACKAGE_VERSION" ]; then
+            VERSION_ARG="-p:PackageVersion=$PACKAGE_VERSION"
+          fi
+
+          dotnet pack "$PACKAGE_PROJECT" \
+            --configuration "$CONFIGURATION" \
+            --no-build \
+            --output "$PACKAGE_OUTPUT" \
+            -p:ContinuousIntegrationBuild=true \
+            -p:IncludeSymbols=true \
+            -p:SymbolPackageFormat=snupkg \
+            $VERSION_ARG
+
+      - name: List package artifacts
+        run: ls -la "$PACKAGE_OUTPUT"
+
+      - name: Upload package artifacts
+        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7
+        with:
+          name: nuget-packages
+          path: |
+            artifacts/packages/*.nupkg
+            artifacts/packages/*.snupkg
+
+      - name: Publish to GitHub Packages
+        run: |
+          for package in "$PACKAGE_OUTPUT"/*.nupkg; do
+            dotnet nuget push "$package" \
+              --source "$GITHUB_PACKAGE_SOURCE" \
+              --api-key "${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}" \
+              --skip-duplicate
+          done
 
-      - name: Publish
+      - name: Publish packages to nuget.org
         run: |
-          dotnet nuget add source --username USERNAME --password ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} --store-password-in-clear-text --name github "https://nuget.pkg.github.com/neovim/index.json"
-          dotnet pack src/NvimClient.API/NvimClient.API.csproj --configuration Release
-          dotnet nuget push "src/NvimClient.API/bin/Release/*.nupkg" --api-key ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} --source github
+          for package in "$PACKAGE_OUTPUT"/*.nupkg; do
+            dotnet nuget push "$package" \
+              --source "$NUGET_ORG_SOURCE" \
+              --api-key "$NUGET_API_KEY" \
+              --skip-duplicate
+          done
+        env:
+          NUGET_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.NUGET_API_KEY }}

comment_data.body:
https://github.blog/changelog/2021-04-20-github-actions-control-permissions-for-github_token/
https://docs.github.com/en/packages/learn-github-packages/about-permissions-for-github-packages#about-scopes-and-permissions-for-package-registries

GitHub changed their permissions model in 2021. There hasn't been a package revision I believe since 2018, so things have simply changed here.

comment_regular_data.body: Note: off the back of this, I want to modernise the tests. These are almost all integration tests, not unit tests. Those should be scoped separately. I'd also want to move to xUnit and off the legacy vstest structure, plus added support for code coverage (codecov?, coverlet?)

>  Note: off the back of this, I want to modernise the tests. These are almost all integration tests, not unit tests. Those should be scoped separately.

that's a feature, not a bug. sometimes unit tests are appropriate, but the main thing is that tests should be fast.
there is usually no reason for a Nvim "integration test" to be slow, so there is no reason to care about "unit tests".

>  want to move to xUnit and off the legacy vstest structure

sure, SGTM

> plus added support for code coverage (codecov?, coverlet)                                                                     

worth a try, but usually ends up being a headache, because all the coverage frameworks are so bad.

> > Note: off the back of this, I want to modernise the tests. These are almost all integration tests, not unit tests. Those should be scoped separately.
> 
> that's a feature, not a bug. sometimes unit tests are appropriate, but the main thing is that tests should be fast. there is usually no reason for a Nvim "integration test" to be slow, so there is no reason to care about "unit tests".

Ah, I wasn't suggesting we remove them at all. Just move the genuine unit tests to a separately named project. Both tests will be run, but the scope of the tests isolated by namespace. Envisioning a `<Product>.UnitTests` and `<Product>.IntegrationTests` project.

Although, the unit tests will completely disappear if we move to `System.Text.Json` because `JsonNamingPolicy` has a case conversion API surface built in (I think since .net 8). Alternatively, I wrote my own case conversion library `ALSI.CaseConversions` that is both faster than the STJ implementation and covers more edge cases (see https://github.com/alsi-lawr/alsi.caseconversions).

> worth a try, but usually ends up being a headache, because all the coverage frameworks are so bad.

I've not had many issues personally, coverlet is just the case of adding a nuget package then you get the coverage report for free as an artifact you can upload to codecov (or github as an artifact).

NB: I do also think it's worth having a talk about renaming the package entirely. `NvimClient.API` isn't really what's being packaged. This is 90% a plugin SDK, not a client/api. I'd want to move to publishing both of the sdk and the actual client separately, and since it's been 8 years since an official release to nuget.org, I think it's the perfect time to revisit some larger changes to the project to modernise it.

I'll elaborate more in separate issues. I have some work that I'm very happy to do for this project as I want to expand on it. I'm working on creating an MCP server in f# for neovim so I can get richer IDE-like support for my agent-term.nvim project I'm trying out, and so would love to do some larger re-architecture piecewise here so the core work can be reusable and within an official collaborative channel.

> Envisioning a  `<Product>.UnitTests`  and `<Product>.IntegrationTests`  project.

the main thing is, I want to avoid over-engineering. These clients, and their codebases, should be VERY small. If they have so many tests that we need to separate things, then something went wrong.

That's the spirit of https://github.com/neovim/nvim.net/issues/26 : delete unnecessary concepts, simplify.

> Although, the unit tests will completely disappear if we move to  System.Text.Json ...

great, exactly the right direction :)

> I've not had many issues personally, coverlet is just the case of adding a nuget package then you get the coverage  report for free as an artifact you can upload to codecov (or github as an artifact).                                

yes but codecov often just doesn't work, fails to load, etc. maybe it improved in recent years, but we tried it for many years...

> This is 90% a plugin SDK, not a client/api.

do you mean "remote plugins"? we want to drop that concept: https://github.com/neovim/nvim.net/issues/26

each Nvim client should be a small runtime that can send/receive requests.

> I'd want to move to publishing both of the sdk   and the actual client separately

why do we need both?

> > This is 90% a plugin SDK, not a client/api.
> 
> do you mean "remote plugins"? we want to drop that concept: #26
> 
> each Nvim client should be a small runtime that can send/receive requests.

Yeah, the actual published package is the `NvimClient.API` package, which is an SDK for writing remote plugins in c#, explicitly loaded through:
```
rplugin/dotnet
remote#host#Register(...)
remote#host#RegisterPlugin(...)
```

> > I'd want to move to publishing both of the sdk and the actual client separately
> 
> why do we need both?

Not for the current published target, but the client is a hidden gem that is useful in more places.
Selfishly, I'm not actually too interested in the sdk itself. I'm much more interested in the generated RPC client that's not a separately published package, as that's useful by itself in a much broader sense, and would be worth investing time (my time) in getting production-quality.

> which is an SDK for writing remote plugins in c#, explicitly loaded through:
> 
> ```
> rplugin/dotnet
> remote#host#Register(...)
> remote#host#RegisterPlugin(...)
> ```

oh yes, let's just drop that entirely. This package gets basically zero usage and "remote plugins" was a mistake in the first place (totally unnecessary), as outlined in https://github.com/neovim/nvim.net/issues/26
@justinmk I believe all the nits are covered, lmk if there's anything else you want changed

title: build: modernize .NET project configuration
